coredata.dc:title: Simultaneous fabrication of carbon nanodots and hydroxyapatite nanoparticles from fish scale for biomedical applications
coredata.dc:creator: Athinarayanan, Jegan
Periasamy, Vaiyapuri Subbarayan
Alshatwi, Ali A.
coredata.dc:description: Fish industries and markets produce large quantities of fish scales, skins, shells, and bone wastes post processing that contaminate the environment and cause health risks in humans. In this context, we have developed a novel and simple integrated process to valorize the Lethrinus lentjan fish scales by fabricate carbon nanodots (CDs) and hydroxyapatite nanoparticles (HA NPs) simultaneously. The fish scale treatment was carried out by hydrothermal method at 280 °C that produced CDs and HA NPs simultaneously. Under hydrothermal treatment, organic and inorganic substances of fish scale is transformed to CDs and HA NPs respectively. As TEM images confirmed that fish scale derived CDs were spherically shaped and ~3 to 15 nm in size. The CDs exhibited excitation-dependent emission in photoluminescence. The HA NPs were ~8 to 12 nm in diameter and ~50 to 100 nm in length with rod shape. Also, HA NPs possess spherical shape nanostructures with 15–50 nm in diameter. Furthermore, we assessed the cytotoxic behavior of synthesized nanostructures using the MTT assay and acridine orange/ethidium bromide (AO/EB) staining. These results showed that synthesized CDs and HA NPs did not cause significant changes in cell viability and morphology, indicating biocompatibility. Additionally, the synthesized CDs and HA NPs were exploited as fluorescent probes for cellular imaging and osteogenic differentiation of stem cells respectively. Overall, the study results indicate that low-cost fish waste was valorized by producing CDs and HA NPs concurrently. The synthesized nanostructures can be applicable for bio-imaging and bone tissue engineering applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fish scale
Carbon nanodots
Hydroxyapatite
MTT assay
Biocompatibility

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of nanostructures from fish scale
Bio-imaging of human mesenchymal stem cells (hMSCs) using CDs
Fabrication of CDs and HA NPs
Cytocompatibility of fish scale derived nanostructures

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of nanostructure fabrication from fish scale and digital photograph of a fish scale and its derived products (Carbon nanodots and Hydroxyapatite nanoparticles).
FTIR spectra of (a) Fish scale and it's derived (b) HA NPs and (c) CDs.
Thermogravimetric curve of (a) Fish scale and its derived (b) CDs and (c) HA NPs.
Fish scale derived CDs (a) UV–Vis spectra (inset figure CDs under a. Day light; b. UV light), (b) Fluorescence spectra with different excitation wavelengths.
XRD pattern of fish scale derived CDs.

(a–c) High resolution transmission electron microscopic images of fish scale derived carbon nanodots and (d) The line profiles of the consistent HRTEM images of CDs. The lattice spacing (d = 0.20 nm) induced from the diffraction plane of graphitic carbon.
X-ray diffraction pattern of (a) Fish scale and (b) fish scale derived HA NPs at 280 °C.
SEM images and corresponding EDX spectra of (a–b) fish scale derived hydroxyapatite nanoparticles at 280 °C.

(a–c) High resolution transmission microscopic images of fish scale derived HA NPs and (d) the line profiles of the consistent HRTEM images of HA NPs. The lattice spacing (d = 0.285 nm) induced from the diffraction plane of hydroxyapatite.
MTT assay cell viability results of CDs and HA NPs on human mesenchymal stem cells (hMSCs) for 24 and 48 h. Data shown as the mean ± SD of each determination performed in triplicates.

Effect of fish scale derived CDs and HA NPs on cellular and nuclear morphology of hMSCs for 24 h. The cellular morphological changes were examined under bright-field microscopy. The nuclear morphological effects in CDs and HA NPs treated and untreated hMSCs for 24 h was assessed using AO/EB staining. Fluorescence microscope images of hMSCs treated with different concentrations of CDs and HA NPs (Control, Dose 1 = 25 μg/mL, Dose 2 = 50 μg/mL and Dose 3 = 100 μg/mL). Scale bar = 100 μm.

Confocal laser microscopic images with different excitation wavelengths of hMSCs after exposure to CDs at the concentration of 25 μg/mL for 6 h. (a) 405 nm (blue), (b) 480 nm (green), (c) 568 nm (red) and (d) merged image. Scale bar = 20 μm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

In vitro alkaline phosphatase (ALP) assay of hMSCs after postinduction of osteogenic differentiation with different doses (0, 12.5 and 25 μg/mL) for 7 and 14 days. Alkaline phosphatase level is increased based on HA NPs concentration and exposure duration.

Alizarin red S staining of different concentration of HA NPs (0, 12.5 and 25 μg/mL) treated and untreated human mesenchymal stem cells after 7 and 14 days. The calcium nodules aggregates were observed in red color. We examine calcium nodules in HA NPs exposed hMSCs. An elevated level of calcium deposition was observed in 25 μg/mL HA NPs exposed cells after 14 days of postinduction. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Simultaneous fabrication of carbon nanodots and hydroxyapatite nanoparticles from fish scale for biomedical applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanobiotechnology and Molecular Biology Research Laboratory, Department of Food Science and Nutrition, College of Food Science and Agriculture, King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanobiotechnology and Molecular Biology Research Laboratory
Department of Food Science and Nutrition
College of Food Science and Agriculture

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanobiotechnology and Molecular Biology Research Laboratory, Department of Food Science and Nutrition, College of Food Science and Agriculture, King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Nanobiotechnology and Molecular Biology Research Lab, Department of Food Science and Nutrition, College of Food and Agricultural Sciences, King Saud University, Riyadh 11451, Saudi Arabia.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanobiotechnology and Molecular Biology Research Lab
Department of Food Science and Nutrition
College of Food and Agricultural Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fish industries and markets produce large quantities of fish scales, skins, shells, and bone wastes post processing that contaminate the environment and cause health risks in humans. In this context, we have developed a novel and simple integrated process to valorize the Lethrinus lentjan fish scales by fabricate carbon nanodots (CDs) and hydroxyapatite nanoparticles (HA NPs) simultaneously. The fish scale treatment was carried out by hydrothermal method at 280 °C that produced CDs and HA NPs simultaneously. Under hydrothermal treatment, organic and inorganic substances of fish scale is transformed to CDs and HA NPs respectively. As TEM images confirmed that fish scale derived CDs were spherically shaped and ~3 to 15 nm in size. The CDs exhibited excitation-dependent emission in photoluminescence. The HA NPs were ~8 to 12 nm in diameter and ~50 to 100 nm in length with rod shape. Also, HA NPs possess spherical shape nanostructures with 15–50 nm in diameter. Furthermore, we assessed the cytotoxic behavior of synthesized nanostructures using the MTT assay and acridine orange/ethidium bromide (AO/EB) staining. These results showed that synthesized CDs and HA NPs did not cause significant changes in cell viability and morphology, indicating biocompatibility. Additionally, the synthesized CDs and HA NPs were exploited as fluorescent probes for cellular imaging and osteogenic differentiation of stem cells respectively. Overall, the study results indicate that low-cost fish waste was valorized by producing CDs and HA NPs concurrently. The synthesized nanostructures can be applicable for bio-imaging and bone tissue engineering applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Biomaterials derived from naturally occurring resources are very attractive and have been employed to design new generation scaffolds for biomedical applications [1]. Further, naturally occurring materials with well-organized hierarchical structures have inspired building of novel structures with multi-functionalization [2,3]. The naturally available biomaterials have received considerable attention from material scientists due to their availability, eco-friendliness, biocompatibility, biodegradability, and cost-effectiveness [1–3]. Several researchers have attempted to derive biomaterials, such as collagen, keratin, cellulose, hyaluronic acid, chitin, chitosan, pectin, calcium phosphate, and biogenic silica, from natural sources. For instance, Song et al. demonstrated that collagen scaffolds were derived from jellyfish [4]. Interestingly, Khanmohammadi et al. reported that hyaluronic acid was extracted from eggshells using a sequential process [5]. Further, Sadhasivam et al. reported that higher molecular mass hyaluronic acid was extracted from the Aetobatus narinari liver [6]. Chitin and chitosan were isolated from three different marine sources: Penaeus kerathurus waste, Carcinus mediterraneus shells, and Sepia officinalis [7]. Additionally, Kaya et al. used six different aquatic invertebrates, including Ranatra linearis, Anax imperator, Hydrophilus piceus, Notonecta glauca, Agabus bipustulatus, and Asellus aquaticus, as precursors of chitin and chitosan production [8]. In our previous study, we isolated biogenic silica nanostructures from rice husk, sugarcane, and millet [9–11].

The fish industry generates a large quantity of waste materials during processing, with 75 million tons of the fish waste generated every year worldwide [12,13]. However, fish waste is hazardous that creates high chemical oxygen demands, biological oxygen demands when dumped into the oceans, and contains pathogenic microbes [14,15]. The management of fish residues is a major issue worldwide. Due to their improper usage, it can cause an environmental nuisance and health problems [16]. Interestingly, the fish waste contains organic (collagen, proteins, and lipids) and inorganic substances (hydroxyapatite), which is a promising precursor material for various high-cost materials fabrication [15–17]. Therefore, utilization of fish waste to produce value-added chemicals can reduce their improper disposal. Previously, few researchers attempted to produce high-value products from fish waste. For example, Chen et al. demonstrated that multicolor fluorescent carbon dots were fabricated from fish scales [18]. Further, Kongsri et al. reported hydroxyapatite nanocrystal preparation from Tilapia nilotica fish scales for selenium adsorption [19]. Hsu et al. extracted collagen from tilapia and evaluated their effect on chondrogenesis of human mesenchymal stem cells [20]. Kumari et al. demonstrated chitosan extraction from crab shells, fish scales, and shrimp shells [21]. Fang et al. analyzed the thermal and cytocompatibility of Carassius auratus fish scales, which are composed of calcium-deficient hydroxyapatite and type I collagen [22]. Moreover, hydroxyapatite nanoparticles were synthesized from Probarbus jullieni scales and Roho labio fish bones [23,24].

Lethrinus lentjan (Red-spot emperor) is commonly distributed in Arabian Gulf. This fish species is usually occurs over rocky bottom and near reefs that has an important viable and societal value. Lethrinus lentjan fish has ctenoid type scales, which is mainly composed of collagen fibrils and hydroxyapatite. Thus, Lethrinus lentjan fish scale could be used as a cheap and easily available source of carbon and hydroxyapatite bio-precursor. To the best of our knowledge, Lethrinus lentjan scales did not exploit as precursor of CDs and HA NPs materials fabrication yet. As per previous studies, hydroxyapatite and carbon nanodots were fabricated from fish waste individually [18,19,22–24]. Bio-refinery based approach of CDs and HA NPs from fish scale waste is still undeveloped. In this context, we developed a novel, innovative and bio-refinery based integrated process to prepare concurrently different kinds of nanostructures (CDs and HA NPs) from fish waste. The fish scale derived nanostructures physicochemical behaviors were analyzed using X-ray diffractometer, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA), and high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM). Additionally, we studied the biocompatibility of the synthesized nanostructures using human mesenchymal stem cells (hMSCs) as an in vitro model.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of nanostructures from fish scale
ce:section.ce:para: The Lethrinus lentjan scales were collected from a local fish market in Riyadh, Kingdom of Saudi Arabia. The obtained fish scales were properly washed with distilled water and dried at room temperature. About 5 g of fish scales were mixed with 250 mL MilliQ water. The mixture was transferred to a hydrothermal autoclave and kept at 280 °C for 3 h. After that, the obtained mixture was cooled to ambient temperature. The obtained mixture contains liquid and solid portions were separated by filtration. The liquid portion containing CDs was purified by a dialysis process and freeze-dried. The obtained solid portion contained HA NPs. The fish scale derived CDs and HA NPs were subjected to further studies.

The synthesized CDs optical properties were analyzed using a UV–Vis-NIR spectrophotometer (Cary 5000, Agilent) and fluorescent spectroscopy (Jasco, FP-750 spectrofluorometer). The X-ray diffraction pattern of the fish scale derived nanostructures was analyzed using an X-ray diffractometer (Bruker diffractometer, Model D8 Advance) and Cu Kα radiation at 40 kV and 100 mA current. The structure and morphological features of the prepared samples were determined using high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The dried samples were dispersed in an aqueous solution under ultrasonication and coated on a carbon-coated copper grid for TEM analyses. The fish scale and it's derived HA NPs elemental composition were assessed using energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy. The functional groups of the samples were investigated using Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra (4000–600 cm−1) of the samples and recorded using a Nicolet 5700 spectrometer.

Effect of the synthesized nanostructures (CDs and HA NPs) on human mesenchymal stem cells viability was assessed by the MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) assay [25]. The cells were plated at a density of 10,000 cells per well in 96-well plates. After overnight incubation, the cells were treated with various concentrations of synthesized nanostructures for 24 and 48 h. After incubation, 20 μL of MTT dye (5 mg/mL) was added in each well. Subsequently, the plates were kept in the dark for 6 h. Following, the media was removed carefully, and the purple colored pellet was dissolved in dimethyl sulfoxide. Finally, the plate was read using a microplate reader

(Promega, Glomax). The average cell survival was calculated from the obtained data using the following formula:
Cell viability
%
=
Mean
OD
of treated cells
Mean
OD
of untreated cells
control
× 100

The synthesized nanostructures influence on cellular and nuclear architecture was assessed [26]. The cells were grown in 12-well plates. After reaching 80–90% confluence, the cells were exposed to different concentrations of prepared nanostructures for 24 h. After incubation, the cells were washed with phosphate buffered saline and examined under brightfield microscope. For nuclear morphology analysis, we used acridine orange/ethidium bromide staining method. The cells were stained with AO/EB dual stain. Subsequently, the cells were observed under fluorescence microscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bio-imaging of human mesenchymal stem cells (hMSCs) using CDs
ce:section.ce:para: For bio-imaging, the hMSCs were grown on glass coverslips. After reaching 70–80% confluence, the cells were treated with fluorescent CDs (25 μg/mL) for 12 h. After treatment, cells were rinsed with phosphate buffered saline and visualized using a confocal microscope (excitation wavelength: 405 nm, 480 nm, and 568 nm).

Around 50,000 of hMSCs per well were plated in 24-well plates and grown for 24 h in normal DMEM. After incubation, the cells were grown in the osteogenic differentiation media (DMEM supplemented with 10 mM β-glycerophosphate, 10 nM dexamethasone [Sigma-Aldrich], 10 nM calcitriol [1α, 25-dihydroxyvitamin D3; Sigma]), 50 μg/mL l-ascorbic acid, 10% FBS and 1% penicillin-streptomycin. Every three days the osteogenic media was replaced by fresh medium. To assess the fish scale derived hydroxyapatite role on osteogenic differentiation, hMSCs were grown with different concentration of HA NPs in osteogenic medium. The control cells were grown in osteogenic medium without HA NPs.

To analyze the alkaline phosphatase activity, different doses of HA NPs (0, 12.5 and 25 μg/mL) were exposed to hMSCs for 7 and 14 days. After incubation, the cells were washed with PBS and fixed with acetone/citrate buffer. After that, the cells were treated with ALP substrate solution (naphthol AS-TR phosphate in 0.1 M Tris buffer, pH 9.0) at room temperature for 1 h. Then, the cells were observed under microscope.

After treatment of HA NPs (0, 12.5 and 25 μg/mL), cells were incubated for 7 and 14 days. Then, the cells were rinsed with PBS and fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde. After that, the cells were washed and stained with 2% alizarin red S for 30 min. Excess stain was removed through water wash and the cells were observed under phase contrast microscope (Axiovert 40 CFL, Carl Zeiss, Germany).

The mean ± SD was calculated from the experimental data, and significant variances from the control were indicated by **p < 0.05. Statistical significance between control and treated groups was analyzed using SPSS software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of CDs and HA NPs
ce:section.ce:para: Fish scale is an important fishery waste, enriched in collagen and hydroxyapatite [27], and is an excellent resource for different kinds of materials. Additionally, fish scale is an inexpensive, easily available, biocompatible, and environmentally benign precursor of different high-value biomaterials [28–30]. Lethrinus lentjan scales' structural features were examined under phase contrast microscope. The microscopic images are shown in Fig. S1 (Supporting data). The structure clearly indicates that Lethrinus lentjan has ctenoid type scales. Generally, ctenoid scales surface layer is composed of hydroxyapatite and the deep layer contains collagen fibrils. Earlier studies demonstrated that ctenoid scale mainly composed of hydroxyapatite and collagen [27]. We have analyzed the elemental composition of fish scale using energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy. The results are shown in Fig. S2 (Supporting data). The EDX spectra clearly indicate the presence of C, O, P and Ca elements in fish scale. The Ca, O and P are corresponding to hydroxyapatite and C is responsible for collagen. This EDX result is similar with earlier studies [27]. Hence, the Lethrinus lentjan scales can act as a sustainable bio-precursor for carbon based materials and hydroxyapatite.

A few studies demonstrated that CDs were synthesized utilizing fish scale as a sustainable precursor through the hydrothermal method [31,32]. Further, some studies have exploited fish scale as a precursor of hydroxyapatite fabrication [28,29,33]. Still, the bio-refinery approach simultaneous fabrication of CDs and HA using fish scale as a precursor is yet unestablished. Thus, in this study, we developed an integrated process to fabricate both CDs and HA NPs concurrently from fish scale. Fig. 1 shows the schematic representation of CDs and HA NPs synthesis from fish scale by the hydrothermal method. The fish scale was treated at 280 °C under pressurized conditions. During this hydrothermal process, fish scales containing organic substances (collagen and other proteins) are transformed to CDs and the remaining inorganic material becomes HA NPs. After treatment, solid and liquid portions were separated by filtration. The liquid portion contained CDs and the solid portion was HA NPs.

The FTIR spectra of the fish scale derived nanostructures are shown in Fig. 2 . As shown in Fig. 2a, FTIR of the fish scale displayed a number of absorption bands at 3393, 3085, 2945, 1635, 1541, 1452, 1390, 1368, 1339, 1237, 1215, 1080, 1034, and 644 cm−1, which corresponded to chitosan, collagen, and hydroxyapatite [26]. The band at 3393 and 644 cm−1 were corresponding to OH stretching and vibrational mode. The peaks at 1635 and 1452 cm−1 were attributed to CO and amide III groups, respectively, due to the presence of collagen and chitosan in fish scale [34]. Furthermore, peaks at 1635, 1541 and 1452 cm−1 are matching to carbonate ions.

Additionally, the peaks at 1034 and 1088 cm−1 were attributed to phosphate groups due to the presence of hydroxyapatite. The FTIR spectra result clearly indicates that fish scales contain chitosan, collagen, and hydroxyapatite. The fish scale derived hydroxyapatite exhibited various absorption peaks around 636, 983, 1027, and 1093 cm−1, as shown in Fig. 2b, which reveals the presence of hydroxyapatite [19,35]. The peak at 636 was assigned as the OH groups vibrational mode. Further, the three peaks around 983, 1027 and 1093 cm−1 were attributed to PO4 3− groups of HA NPs [19,35]. Interestingly, the carbonate ions responsible peaks are missed in the fish scale derived HA NPs. Fig. 2c displays the fish scale derived CDs FTIR spectrum. The characteristic band observed at 3310 cm−1 was assigned to the NH vibration [31]. The absorption band at 2924 cm−1 and 1635 was attributed to CH3 and CO stretching vibrations. Additionally, the peaks at 1452 cm−1 and 1395 cm−1 could be attributed to the amide III CN stretching vibrations. The two bands at 1247 and 1113 cm−1 corresponded to CN and CO groups stretching vibration [31]. Further, the peak around 1325 cm−1 could be assigned as CH2 . These results clearly indicate that CDs surfaces possess N and O functionalities.

Fig. 3 depicts the thermal behavior of fish scale and its derived nanostructures. Around 15% of weight loss examined in between 100 and 280 °C in fish scale due to organic materials degradation. In between 280 and 400 °C, around 60% of weight losses observed in fish scale due to the organic substances. In fish scale derived hydroxyapatite, we observed 4% of weight loss due to elimination of water. These results clearly indicate that fish scale derived hydroxyapatite is highly pure. Furthermore, the thermal gravimetric analysis of CDs shows the gradual weight loss in between the temperature of 100–400 °C, which attributed that CDs surfaces covalently joined with chemical groups.

Fig. 4a depicts the UV–Vis spectra of fish scale derived CDs. The spectra exhibited a sharp peak at 280 nm, whereas the peaks were attributed to the n–π* transition of the carbonyl groups. The inset digital photograph of Fig. 4a indicates the CDs displaying bright blue fluorescence under UV light at λex = 365 nm. Similarly, previous studies reported that food products derived carbon nanodots exhibited blue fluorescent under UV light [36].

Further, we studied the fluorescence behaviors of synthesized CDs by changing the excitation wavelength from 280 nm to 520 nm. Fig. 4b depicts the fluorescence spectra of CDs derived from fish scale. These results revealed that CDs show excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior that is habitual in fluorescent carbon nanostructures [37]. When the excitation wavelength increased, a red shift was observed in the fluorescence spectra. Moreover, the excitation wavelength at 340 nm obtained a strong emission peak. Auspiciously, the excitation-dependent fluorescent property can be suitable in multicolor patterning applications. These properties are caused by the distribution of different sizes of CDs with different surface functional groups [38].

Fig. 5 depicts the XRD pattern of fish scale derived CDs. The XRD spectra showed a broad peak at 22° of 2 theta value, which corresponded to (002) plane of graphitic carbon. The CDs structure and morphology were investigated using transmission electron microscopy. As shown in Fig. 6 , the high resolution transmission electron microscopic (HRTEM) image of CDs. The HRTEM image of CDs revealed that CDs were well dispersed and spherically shaped dots with ~3 to 15 nm in diameter. The HRTEM images (Fig. 6c and d) clearly shows CDs lattice spacing with the interplanar distance of ~0.20 to 0.22 nm, which can be indexed to the (102) diffraction plane of graphitic carbon [39].
The fish scale derived HA NPs crystalline nature was studied using X-ray diffractometer.

Fig. 7 shows the XRD pattern of fish scale and its derived HA NPs. The fish scale shows the peak around 20.02 and 31.8, which is corresponding to polymeric materials and hydroxyapatite respectively. Similarly, Paul et al. demonstrated that broad peaks were observed in Catla catla fish scale due to presence of hydroxyapatite in collagen matrix [35]. The XRD pattern exhibited a number of peaks attributed to standard JCPDS 00-009-0432 corresponding to HA NPs (Fig. 7b). The fish scale derived HA NPs had (002), (102), (210), (211), (112), (300), (202), (301), (310), (302), (400), (222), (213), (321), (402) and (004) planes. The XRD results clearly indicate that HA NPs is a hexagonal crystalline structure [23,40]. At 280 °C hydrothermal treatment, the collagen and other proteins were transformed to carbon-based materials. Thus, the HA NPs peaks became highly intense and sharp.

Fig. 8 depicts the SEM images and EDS spectra of the fish scale derived HA NPs. The result clearly indicates that fish scale derived materials contain calcium and phosphate ions, which revealed that derived materials are HA NPs. The elemental composition of the fish scale derived hydroxyapatite Ca/P ratio is shown in Fig. 8. We found the average of Ca/P weight ratio in fish scale derived HA NPs as 2.33. The results suggested that the fish scale derived HA NPs Ca/P ratio is higher than the bone regeneration enhanced composite Ca/P stoichiometric ratio (1.67) [23,40,41].

The morphological feature of the HA NPs was studied using HRTEM, and results are shown in Fig. 9 . From HRTEM analysis, we confirmed that HA NPs possesses agglomeration of ~8 to 12 nm in diameter and ~50 to 100 nm in length rod-shaped nanostructures. Also, we observed spherical shaped particles with ~15 to 50 nm in diameter of HA NPs. The HRTEM image (Fig. 9d) clearly indicates lattice fringes with d-spacings of 0.285 nm, which is corresponding to (211) diffraction plane of HA NPs [42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytocompatibility of fish scale derived nanostructures
ce:section.ce:para: Toxic effects cause destructive cellular changes or require intervention with biochemical parameters due to changes induced by physical or chemical agents. Thus, materials used in biological applications must possess insignificant toxic effects or are non-toxic in biological systems. Currently, few studies have demonstrated that nanostructured materials possess adverse toxic effects in biological systems [36,43,44].

At this juncture, we assessed a fish scale derived CDs and HA NPs toxicology pattern using hMSCs as an in vitro model. The different concentrations of nanostructures were exposed to hMSCs for 24 and 48 h. Fig. 10 depicts the effect of CDs and HA NPs on cell viability of hMSCs. At a high concentration, CDs-treated cells showed a 4 to 7% decrease in cell viability. Further, we found a 5 to 10% decline in cell viability of HA NPs-treated cells. However, no significant cell viability changes were observed in CDs- and HA NPs-treated hMSCs, up to 200 μg/mL. These results indicate that fish scale derived nanostructures are non-toxic and biocompatible. Moreover, the materials can be applicable in the biomedical sector.

Additionally, we analyzed cell and nuclear morphological changes in CDs- and HA NPs-treated hMSCs. Fig. 11 shows the effect of CDs and HA NPs on hMSC cellular and nuclear morphology. The cells were healthy and of elongated needle shape after exposure to CDs or HA NPs, when compared to untreated control cells. Further, AO/EB stained images showed nanostructures-treated and untreated cells had green colored, intact, healthy nuclei. We did not observe any changes in nuclear architecture. These results revealed that these materials are non-toxic and biocompatible to human mesenchymal stem cells. Similarly, earlier studies demonstrated that biocompatible materials did not alter any cellular and nuclear architecture of stem cells [10,45]. For instance, Athinarayanan et al. reported that biogenic silica nanostructures exhibited excellent biocompatibility on human mesenchymal stem cells up to 400 μg/mL [10]. Similarly, Alshatwi et al. demonstrated that rice husk derived silica nanoparticles do not induce any toxicity on human lung fibroblast cells [45]. Our previous studies reported that lignocellulose derived nanostructures including carbon nanodots, lignin nanoparticles and cellulose nanostructures shows good biocompatibility on hMSCs up to 200 μg/mL [25,46].

Due to excitation dependent fluorescence behavior and biocompatibility of CDs, this can be used as a fluorescent probe for cell imaging. We evaluated the fish scale derived CDs for cellular bio-imaging. For bio-imaging applications, the cells were stained using 25 μg/mL of CDs. The cell morphology was observed under confocal fluorescence microscopy at different excitations with a 405 nm, 480 nm, and 568 nm laser (Fig. 12 ). The CDs could readily enter the cytoplasm and nucleus through the cell membrane. Thus, we observed the cells under confocal microscope at different excitations due to multicolor emission.

The fish scale derived biocompatible HA NPs osteogenic potential was assessed using alkaline phosphatase assay and alizarin red S staining. Alkaline phosphatase is a vital early marker for osteogenic differentiation, which is present in surfaces of osteoblast membranes. The hMSCs were exposed to HA NPs for 7 and 14 days. Fig. 13a shows the ALP staining of the hMSCs. On postinduction day 7 and 14, high ALP staining was examined in HA NPs exposed cells compared to control cells. Also, more ALP staining was observed in high dose of HA NPs exposed cells compared to low dose. The results clearly indicate that HA NPs induced the osteogenic differentiation by dose-dependent manner. These studies suggest that HA NPs promotes hMSCs differentiation to osteoblast. Interestingly, the activity of ALP is high on postinduction day 14 at high dose of HA NPs exposed cells. Similarly, earlier studies demonstrated that biogenic silica nanostructures treatment increased the ALP activity in hMSCs [47].

Moreover, we have performed the alizarin red s staining after postinduction day 7 and 14 to analyze the impact of HA NPs on hMSCs osteogenic differentiation. The calcium nodules formation was identified using Alizarin red stain in osteoblasts. The stain is reacting with calcium ions to form alizarin red s – calcium ion complex. The microscopic images of the alizarin red s stained cells shows more calcium nodules like structure with accumulated cells immobilized in mineralized matrix (Fig. 13b ). We observed the different levels of calcium mineralization in HA NPs treated and control cells. The high density of calcium nodules was examined in HA NPs treated cells compared to control cells. When the HA NPs dose level is increased the calcium nodules also increased. This result suggests that HA NPs stimulates calcium mineralization in hMSCs. Our results clearly suggest that high dose of HA NPs increases the level of alkaline phosphatase and calcium nodules formation compared to control and low dose HA NPs treated cells. Similarly, plant derived silica nanostructures enhanced the calcium deposition in hMSCs [47,48]. Our findings indicate that fish scale derived HA NPs can stimulate osteogenic differentiation in hMSCs. In future studies, we will be assessing the HA NPs role in osteogenic differentiation at molecular level in in vitro and in vivo.

The fish scale derived biocompatible hydroxyapatite could be used for in bone tissue engineering applications. Overall, our study results suggest an integrated process, which can transform inexpensive, sustainable, and eco-friendly fish scale into high-value fluorescent probe CDs and non-toxic HA NPs simultaneously. Further, this process could be applicable to valorize all kinds of fish scales.

In this investigation, for the first time, simultaneously we have fabricated carbon nanodots (CDs) and hydroxyapatite nanoparticles (HA NPs) using Lethrinus lentjan fish scale as a bio-precursor. The developed integrated process is suitable to valorize the various fish scales. The fish scale derived carbon nanodots have 8–12 nm in size and it exhibited excitation-dependent fluorescent behavior. The TEM and TGA results revealed that of HA NPs have rod-shaped agglomerated particles and high thermal stability. The cell viability and morphological assessment results suggested that CDs and HA NPs do not induce cell death and morphological changes in human mesenchymal stem cells. Our findings clearly suggested that fish scale derived materials are biocompatible and non-toxic, which can be applicable for bio-imaging and bone tissue engineering applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support of the Researchers Supporting Project, King Saud University, Saudi Arabia (Grant No: RSP-2020-178). We acknowledge the Researchers Support & Services Unit, Deanship of Scientific Research, King Saud University, Saudi Arabia for their English editing. The partial work of this manuscript has achieved a US patent (US 10383976).
This work was supported by the Researchers Supporting Project (RSP-2020/178), King Saud University, Saudi Arabia.

Jegan Athinarayanan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Data curation, Writing- Original draft preparation. Vaiyapuri Subbarayan Periasamy: Data curation, Writing-Original draft preparation. Ali A Alshatwi: Visualization, Investigation, Supervision, Writing- Reviewing and Editing.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2020.111313.
